# Benchmark: one skill, seven targets

<!-- generated by kitbash — run: node packages/cli/scripts/benchmark.mjs -->

Kitbash compiles one source skill to every agent's native format. This measures what that output actually costs, in tokens, per target — run on committed fixtures through the real `kitbash compile` pipeline.

Two costs matter:

- **Loaded** — tokens the agent reads when the skill is in play.
- **Standing** — tokens sitting in the context window *every session, before the skill is even invoked*. Lazy targets keep only a stub; eager targets carry the whole body.

Token counts are estimates (~4 chars/token), the same estimator the compiler enforces budgets with. Reproduce with `node packages/cli/scripts/benchmark.mjs`.

## `prereview` — manifested (budget 1500, lazy)

| Target | Loaded (tok) | Standing (tok) | Loading |
|---|--:|--:|:--|
| claude-code | 515 | 40 | lazy |
| cursor | 516 | 40 | lazy |
| copilot | 492 | 492 | eager |
| cline | 486 | 486 | eager |
| windsurf | 486 | 486 | eager |
| gemini | 507 | 507 | eager |
| agentsmd | 507 | 507 | eager |

**Standing tax:** ~40 tokens on a lazy target vs ~492 on an eager one — about **12× per session** for the identical skill. A team running four agents pays that gap four times over, forever, unless something measures it.

## `review-checklist` — bare / unmanifested (no budget)

| Target | Loaded (tok) | Standing (tok) | Loading |
|---|--:|--:|:--|
| claude-code | 880 | 19 | lazy |
| cursor | 879 | 19 | lazy |
| copilot | 865 | 865 | eager |
| cline | 859 | 859 | eager |
| windsurf | 859 | 859 | eager |
| gemini | 885 | 885 | eager |
| agentsmd | 885 | 885 | eager |

**Standing tax:** ~19 tokens on a lazy target vs ~865 on an eager one — about **46× per session** for the identical skill. A team running four agents pays that gap four times over, forever, unless something measures it.

## Why this is the pitch

No other format has a field for this. Kitbash measures it at compile time, warns when a lazy-authored skill lands on an eager target, and fails the build under `--strict`. The copy-per-agent status quo pays the eager cost everywhere and never sees the number.
